nativeRange.habitat: Brackish estuaries, muddy bays, river mouths, and coastal swamps; found in mud-bottomed intertidal and subtidal zones with soft substrate; occasionally enters freshwater and open marine water

decorPreferences: deep soft sand substrate (5+ cm) for partial burrowing
lengths of PVC pipe or plastic tubing as bolt-holes
smooth bogwood or driftwood for cover
low or no current — gentle filtration output only
dim or subdued lighting to reduce stress

diet.notes: Micro/filter-feeder by nature — scoops mouthfuls of substrate and filters out micro-organisms, algae, and detritus. In captivity accepts finely crushed or sinking micro-pellets, algae wafers, spirulina flakes, frozen baby brine shrimp, frozen bloodworms, frozen tubifex, and daphnia. Foods must be small enough to pass through the sieving gill rakers. Sharp teeth are for scraping algae and biofilm, not attacking tankmates. Feed once daily or in small amounts twice daily; target-feed near hiding spots at lights-out as the species is largely nocturnal. Vegetable matter (spirulina, algae wafers) is important for long-term health.

goodWith: other large peaceful brackish fish (figure-eight puffers kept well-fed)
avoidWith: aggressive or nippy tankmates that will harass the slow-moving goby
fast competitive feeders that outcompete at feeding time
conspecifics in small tanks (territorial aggression toward own species)
small fish or invertebrates that may be accidentally ingested during filter-feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Gobioides broussonnetii; shows eel-like body profile and iridescent violet colouration.

summary: The Violet Goby is a large, eel-like brackish-water goby from Atlantic American estuaries, reaching 60+ cm. Despite its formidable teeth and dragon-like appearance it is a gentle filter-feeder requiring specialist brackish setups (SG 1.005–1.012). Frequently mislabelled as a freshwater fish — a leading cause of early mortality in captivity.
careNotes: The Violet Goby (also widely sold as Dragon Goby or Dragon Fish) is one of the largest gobies in the Western Atlantic, reaching up to 62 cm in the wild. Despite an imposing appearance — elongated eel-like body, prominent teeth, iridescent violet-silver colouring — it is a docile, slow-moving filter-feeder entirely unsuited to the community freshwater aquaria in which it is most commonly sold. The single most important care requirement is brackish water: a specific gravity of SG 1.005–1.012 is essential for long-term health. Fish maintained in pure freshwater may survive for months but invariably decline and die prematurely from osmotic stress and suppressed immunity. This species is native to Atlantic estuaries from South Carolina (USA) south to northern Brazil, inhabiting muddy bays, river mouths, and tidal swamps — environments with fluctuating but consistently measurable salinity. A minimum tank of 450 litres with a 120 cm footprint is recommended for a single adult; the tank must be tightly covered as the species is an escape artist. Substrate should be 5+ cm of fine soft sand — the goby spends much of its time partially buried or pressing its ventral surface into the substrate, and coarse gravel damages the belly and causes abrasions. Provide multiple hiding places: lengths of plastic pipe, cave arrangements of smooth rock, and pieces of driftwood all serve well. Filtration should be gentle; strong current stresses these fish. The species is largely nocturnal; lighting should be dim or photoperiod should include a dusk period. Feeding requires care — all foods must be small enough to be sieved through the gill rakers (micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, baby brine shrimp, bloodworms, algae wafers). Feed near hides at dusk. Do not house with aggressive or nippy species that may fin-bite the long trailing fins. Water quality must be stable; the species is sensitive to ammonia and elevated nitrate. Weekly partial water changes of 10–15% are recommended. The confusingly similar-looking Gobioides peruanus is sometimes sold under the same names but reportedly tolerates freshwater better — identification is difficult in the trade.
breedingNotes: Not reported in captivity. In the wild, spawning is presumed to occur in estuarine environments with some shift toward higher salinity. Sexual dimorphism is subtle: males have a long, pointed genital papilla; females have a short, blunt, yellowish one. No aquarium breeding protocol has been documented. Breeding difficulty is listed as "not in captivity" given the absence of verified reports.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Gobioides broussonnetii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 62.5 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 7.5–9.0, hardness 10–25°H, salinity SG 1.005–1.015, minimum tank 450 L (48×24×24 in), diet (algae/detritus/frozen foods), native range (S USA to Brazil including Caribbean), breeding (not achieved in captivity), and substrate/decor requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Gobioides broussonnetii
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum size 55.3 cm TL, temperature range 14–32°C (optimal 23–28°C), salinity tolerance 0–25.5 ppt, native range (Charleston SC southward to Florida, Gulf of Mexico coast, Caribbean coast of Colombia east to Venezuela, Surinam, Guyana, French Guiana, and Brazil), taxonomy (family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes), demersal/estuarine habitat. Trophic level 3.8.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Violet goby
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range, habitat (brackish estuaries/river mouths), diet (filamentous algae, micro-invertebrates scraped with teeth), sexual dimorphism (genital papilla), IUCN status (Least Concern), eel-like body morphology. Noted wild maximum 61 cm, captive specimens seldom exceed 38 cm.

additional.name: Aquarium Source — Dragon Fish Goby Care
additional.notes: Hobbyist-facing care guide. Confirmed salinity SG 1.006–1.008, temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.5–8.5, hardness 10–20 dKH, lifespan ~10 years, minimum tank 4 ft length for one specimen, feeding strategy (target-feed near hides), sensitivity to ammonia/nitrate, disease susceptibility (fin rot, columnaris), and partly-nocturnal behaviour.

additional.name: FishLore — Care Sheet for Dragon Goby (forum)
additional.notes: Community experience confirming brackish requirement (SG 1.005–1.010 recommended by experienced keepers), escape-risk warning, sand substrate necessity, pipe/tube hiding spots, and feeding approach for this micro-filter species.

fish.breedingNotes: No verified captive breeding on record. Wild spawning details undocumented. Sexual dimorphism visible via genital papilla (male: long/pointed; female: short/blunt/yellowish). No aquarium protocol exists.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Sold as freshwater but requires BRACKISH (SG 1.005-1.012) for long-term health. Eel-like 60cm+ goby. Specialist large brackish setup.
